html:
XLVI

I went down slowly towards the bottom of the mountain gorge. Only the distant cry of the gurr√≠es and the murmur of the river disturbed the silence of the woods. My heart was saying farewell to each spotвБ†вАФto each tree by the path, to each brook I crossed.

Seated on the riverвАЩs bank, I thought of those good souls who had shed tears at my departure. My own fell to mingle with the fleeing watersвБ†вАФfleeing from me like the happy days of those six months.

In half an hour I reached the house, and went to my motherвАЩs sewing-room. Only she and Emma were there; more than an hour passed before I asked for Mar√≠a.

вАЬWe were with her in the oratory,вАЭ said Emma. вАЬShe went to the storeroom afterwards. She cannot know that you have come back.вАЭ

It had never happened before that I should return to the house and not see Mar√≠a within a few minutes. I was much afraid that she had fallen again into that dejection which so discouraged me; yet I had seen her making incessant efforts to overcome it during the past week.

An hour afterwards, Juan knocked at my door to call me to dinner. As I went out I found Mar√≠a leaning against the grated window of the sewing-room.

вАЬMamma didnвАЩt call you,вАЭ said the laughing boy.

вАЬWho taught you to tell falsehoods, then?вАЭ I replied; вАЬMar√≠a will not forgive you for that.вАЭ

вАЬShe was the one who sent me to do it,вАЭ said Juan, pointing at her.

I turned to Mar√≠a to see if it were true. I did not need to ask her; her smile confessed it. Her bright eyes expressed the old peaceful happiness which our love had taken from them. Upon her cheeks was the quick blush which used to beautify them in our childish sports. She wore a white dress and her braids fell in waves over the graceful skirt at the slightest movement she made.

вАЬWhy are you so sad and secluded?вАЭ she asked me. вАЬI have not been so today.вАЭ

вАЬI am not sure of that,вАЭ I replied, so as to have a pretext for going up close to look at her through the grating between us.

She lowered her eyes, pretending to tie over again the strings of her blue silk apron. Then she crossed her hands behind her back, and leaned against one of the window-shutters, saying, вАЬIsnвАЩt it true?вАЭ

вАЬI doubted it, but since you have succeeded in deceiving meвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬDeceiving you, indeed! Is it a good thing for you to go and shut yourself up, when you are going away after one night more?вАЭ

вАЬI am glad to see you so courageous. And is it a good thing for me not to be able to see you for two hours after coming home?вАЭ

вАЬWhat sort of an hour is twelve for coming down the mountain? Besides, I have been very busy. I saw you, though, as you were coming down. To prove it, you did not have your rifle, and Mayo was lagging far behind.вАЭ

вАЬSo you have been very busy. What have you been doing?вАЭ

вАЬOh, everything. Something good, and something bad.вАЭ

вАЬTell me what.вАЭ

вАЬI have prayed a good deal.вАЭ

вАЬEmma told me that already; what else have you done?вАЭ

вАЬThe bad thing?вАЭ

вАЬYes, the bad thing.вАЭ

вАЬWill you go to pray with me tonight if I tell you?вАЭ

вАЬYes.вАЭ

вАЬBut you must not tell mamma; she will be angry.вАЭ

вАЬI promise not to tell her.вАЭ

вАЬI have been ironing.вАЭ

вАЬYou?вАЭ

вАЬOf course, I.вАЭ

вАЬBut how did you manage it?вАЭ

вАЬWithout mammaвАЩs knowing it.вАЭ

вАЬBut why do you need to roughen your hands with such work?вАЭ

вАЬAh! I wanted you to take away your finest shirts ironed by me. ArenвАЩt you glad? DonвАЩt you thank me?вАЭ

вАЬBut who taught you to iron? How in the world did you think of doing it?вАЭ

вАЬOne day when Juan √Бngel brought back some shirts, saying that his master did not like them, I told Marcelina that I would help her to do them better.вАЭ

вАЬI thank you a thousand times for all your pains. I never imagined that you had strength enough to manage an iron.вАЭ

вАЬIf you take a very small one, and carefully wrap up the handle in a handkerchief, it cannot hurt your hands.вАЭ

вАЬLet me see yours.вАЭ

вАЬWhy? they are perfectly right.вАЭ

вАЬShow them to me.вАЭ

вАЬThey are just as they always are.вАЭ

вАЬI doubt it.вАЭ

вАЬLook at them.вАЭ

I took them in mine, and stroked the palms, soft as satin.

вАЬIs anything wrong with them?вАЭ she asked.

вАЬAs mine are roughвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

вАЬThey donвАЩt feel so to me. What did you do on the mountain?вАЭ

вАЬI suffered a great deal. I never thought they would feel so sorry about my going, nor that it would grieve me so sincerely to say goodbye to themвБ†вАФespecially to Braulio and the girls.вАЭ

вАЬWhat did they say?вАЭ

вАЬPoor things! They couldnвАЩt say anything, they cried so. But donвАЩt you feel sad. I should not have told you of this. Let me remember you in our last hours together, as you were today, cheerful, almost happy.вАЭ

вАЬYes,вАЭ she said, wiping her eyes, вАЬI want to be like that. Tomorrow! Only one more day! But it will be Sunday, and we shall be together all day. We will read together. And you must tell me how you most like to see me dressed.вАЭ

вАЬJust as you are now.вАЭ

вАЬVery well. There they come to call us to dinner. Goodbye.вАЭ

She used to take leave of me in that way, although we were to be together afterwards; for it seemed to her, as it did to me, that when we were with all the family we were separated from each other.
